source.name: @fake_hockey_patches
source.note: The hobby's leading community feed documenting swapped patches. We link out and credit the source rather than rehosting their photographs.

intro: A "patch swap" is when the memorabilia window on a game-used or manufactured patch card is opened and the original swatch is replaced with a more desirable one — a multi-color patch, a logo, a nameplate letter — to inflate value. Because the card looks factory-fresh, swaps are hard to catch without a reference photo of the card as issued.

redFlags.title: Patch doesn't match the manufacturer photo
redFlags.detail: Panini, Upper Deck and Topps photograph most premium patch cards at production. Compare your card to the seller's or manufacturer's original image — a different swatch is the clearest tell.

redFlags.title: Patch is too clean or too perfect
redFlags.detail: Genuine game-used swatches are usually single-color and unremarkable. A pristine 3–4 color logo patch on a low-numbered rookie is a red flag worth verifying.

redFlags.title: Window seal or plastic looks disturbed
redFlags.detail: Look for lifted edges, glue residue, tool marks, bubbling, or a patch that sits proud of the window. Reseals rarely match the factory finish exactly.

redFlags.title: Patch size or shape fills the window unnaturally
redFlags.detail: Swapped patches are often cut to fit and can look crammed, off-center, or oriented differently than the die-cut window intends.

redFlags.title: Serial number vs. rarity mismatch
redFlags.detail: Cross-check the print run in the checklist. A ‘1-of-1 patch’ that shows up in multiple listings, or a patch grander than the set warrants, deserves scrutiny.

redFlags.title: No grading, or a cracked-and-reslabbed history
redFlags.detail: Raw premium patches — or ones removed from a graded holder — carry more swap risk. Reputable third-party authentication of the patch itself is the safest path.
